title: Dog Dragging Hind Legs: Causes, Emergency Signs & Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is dragging their hind legs, it is a medical emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is vital for paralysis and mobility loss.
content_html:
TL;DR: A dog dragging its hind legs is a high-priority medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary care to prevent permanent paralysis. Providing a video of the behavior to the clinic can help the triage team quickly prepare for your pet's specific diagnostic needs.

What does it mean when my dog suddenly starts dragging their hind legs?

This condition, known as hind-limb paralysis or paresis, occurs when the communication between the spinal cord and the rear legs is interrupted. It may manifest as:

  • Dragging the legs
  • 'Knuckling' (walking on the tops of the paws)
  • A complete inability to stand or move the lower half of the body

Is my dog dragging their back legs considered a medical emergency?

Yes, this is a high-priority medical emergency where time is of the essence. Sudden loss of mobility often points to serious neurological or vascular issues, such as:

  • Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
  • Spinal trauma
  • A 'saddle thrombus' (blood clot)

The faster a dog receives medical intervention, the higher the likelihood of preserving nerve function and preventing permanent paralysis.

How does providing a video of my dog's movement help the veterinary triage team?

When you contact an emergency clinic, providing a short video of your dog attempting to move can be incredibly helpful for the triage team. A video allows the veterinarian to:

  • See the specific nature of the gait and level of motor control
  • Determine whether the issue appears neurological or orthopedic
  • Prioritize your pet's case based on severity
  • Prepare necessary diagnostic tools, like an MRI or CT scan, before you even arrive

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Peripheral nerve injuries, particularly those affecting the sciatic nerve or its branches, are common causes of hind limb dragging in dogs. These injuries can result from pelvic fractures, iatrogenic damage during femoral intramedullary pin placement, or injection of irritating substances near the nerve. Damage to the proximal sciatic nerve can lead to the dog bearing little to no weight on the affected limb, dragging the dorsal surface of the paw. Avulsion of caudal nerve roots can cause loss of sensation on the caudal surface of the limb, with variable loss on the cranial surface. In cases of complete avulsion, amputation may be necessary due to self-mutilation or trauma from dragging.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1244)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of hind leg dragging?
faqs.answer: The most frequent causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), spinal cord injuries, fibrocartilaginous embolism (FCE), and in older dogs, degenerative myelopathy.

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. If your dog is unable to walk or dragging their legs, they should be seen by an emergency veterinarian immediately to prevent permanent damage.

faqs.question: Should I give my dog pain medication at home?
faqs.answer: Do not administer any human or leftover pet medications, as these can interfere with emergency treatments or be toxic. Keep your dog calm and confined until you reach the vet.
